     The Numbers Business Report

Friday Night Lights (2004)

Theatrical Performance
Domestic Box Office $61,255,921Details
International Box Office $694,849
Worldwide Box Office $61,950,770
Further financial details...

Synopsis

A town for sale, Odessa, Texas has seen better days—the financial bust evident in its boarded-up shops and broken lives. Yet one hope sustains the community where, once a week during the fall, the town and its dreams come alive beneath the dazzling and disorienting Friday night lights when the Permian High Panthers take to the field. In a city where economic uncertainty has eroded the spirit of its inhabitants, nearly everyone seeks comfort in the religion of the Friday night ritual, where the unfulfilled dreams of an entire community are shifted onto the shoulder pads of a team of high-school athletes.

Metrics

Opening Weekend:$20,269,025 (33.1% of total gross)
Legs:3.02 (domestic box office/biggest weekend)
Domestic Share:98.9% (domestic box office/worldwide)
Production Budget:$30,000,000 (worldwide box office is 2.1 times production budget)
Theater counts:2,667 opening theaters/3,004 max. theaters, 5.4 weeks average run per theater
Infl. Adj. Dom. BO $111,562,711

Movie Details

Domestic Releases: October 8th, 2004 (Wide) by Universal
International Releases: March 11th, 2005 (Wide) (Australia)
Video Release: January 18th, 2005 by Universal Home Entertainment
MPA Rating: PG-13 for thematic issues, sexual content, language, some teen drinking and rough sports action
Running Time: 118 minutes
Cast, crew, or production detail: Epilogue
Geographical setting: Texas
Plot point: Football, Inspirational Coach
Subgenre: Sports Drama
Time period setting: 1980s
Source:Based on Factual Book/Article
Genre:Drama
Production Method:Live Action
Creative Type:Dramatization
Production Countries: United States
Languages: English

Synopsis

A town for sale, Odessa, Texas has seen better days—the financial bust evident in its boarded-up shops and broken lives. Yet one hope sustains the community where, once a week during the fall, the town and its dreams come alive beneath the dazzling and disorienting Friday night lights when the Permian High Panthers take to the field. In a city where economic uncertainty has eroded the spirit of its inhabitants, nearly everyone seeks comfort in the religion of the Friday night ritual, where the unfulfilled dreams of an entire community are shifted onto the shoulder pads of a team of high-school athletes.
